text: The surgeon stands on the right side of the patient as a thirty-degree laparoscope is inserted through the umbilical port. The liver is carefully examined, and the peritoneal surface is explored for signs of tumor metastasis. The sigmoid tumor is identified, and the proximal resection margin is marked with a clip. Any free peritoneal fluid is collected for cytology, and the uterus may be suspended with a transfixed stitch for better exposure.

The surgeon identifies several anatomical structures, including the left ureter, inferior mesenteric vein, left colic vein, left colic artery, left kidney, left renal vein, and left gonadal vein. The inferior pancreatic margin is also identified as the dissection proceeds upwards.

The surgeon follows the left colic vessels to reach the left colic flexure, observing the gastric body through the transverse mesocolon. The aortic plane is located, and the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery is identified. Lymph nodes are visible near the area. On the right side of the aorta, the inferior vena cava is visualized. As the view proceeds downwards, the promontorium is visualized, along with the mesosigmoid, left ureter, and left gonadal vessels.

The surgeon visualizes the common iliac artery, external iliac artery, and hypogastric artery. The right ureter is seen following the superior aspect of the hypogastric artery. The right gonadal vessels are clearly visible as they cross the right external iliac vessels. The right external iliac artery and vein, right round ligament of the uterus, right gonadal vessels, right uterine tube, right ureter, and right inguinal ligament are all clearly visualized.

The surgeon identifies the path of the left common iliac vessels at the aortic bifurcation. Bilaterally, the ureters are traced as they enter the pelvis, crossing the bifurcation of the common iliac vessels. The close proximity of the left ureter to the proximal tract of the inferior mesenteric artery is noted. The intermesenteric plexus is identified, and the peritoneum is divided between the inferior mesenteric vein and the aorta to facilitate visualization and division of the atolletes fasciae, lowering the left ureter away from the inferior mesenteric artery for safer pursuit of the artery.
